room.name: outside Freedom Mansions.
room.description: The building looms grey and dilapidated behind you, plastered with posters of Big Brother. The air is acrid with smoke from nearby chimneys. The street stretches eastward toward the Prole quarters.

room.name: a Party-controlled street.
room.description: This wide street is cracked and grimy. Telescreens mounted on poles blare Party propaganda, their metallic voices echoing off empty shopfronts. Few people linger here; the patrols make sure of that.

room.name: the entrance to Green Park.
room.description: Once a pleasant green space, Green Park now lies untended and grim. The grass grows wild in patches, littered with broken bottles and scraps of paper. Groups of men huddle together, watching strangers with suspicion. Patrols cut across the paths at irregular intervals, their boots heavy on the gravel. To the east, the broken facades of Victory Square loom. A group of guards blocks the exit through to Victory Square.

room.name: deep in Green Park.
room.description: Tall, unkempt grass and thickets of bare trees block the light. In the shadows, shapes move and voices whisper. A rusted bench sits beneath a leaning lamppost that has not worked for years. You feel exposed here.

room.name: a ruined bandstand.
room.description: The remains of a bandstand rise like broken teeth, its roof long collapsed. This part of the park feels quieter, as if it's been forgotten. A lamppost stands next to the bandstand, paint peeling and rusted beneath. It's been many years since the lamp was last lit. A pane of cracked glass dangles precariously from the rusted lamp housing, catching the light. It looks like a stiff breeze would blow it down. To the South lies Freedom Square but the route is blocked by patrols.

room.name: a crumbling fountain in Green Park.
room.description: The broken remains of a fountain stand here, cracked and dry. The stone basin is filled with debris and rainwater. A group of Prole youths linger nearby, their eyes darting toward you with unfriendly curiosity.

room.name: a shadowed path near a service grate.
room.description: The path narrows here, flanked by wild grass and broken trees. A rusted iron grate lies half-buried in the dirt to the south. Something heavy lies in the grass nearby. Faint voices carry from the east.

room.name: a Patrol Crossing in Green Park.
room.description: The gravel path is cut by a Party patrol route. Guards march in pairs, blocking the way east through to Victory Square. Their eyes are sharp for anyone lingering too long. A telescreen is mounted on a tree, its flickering face watching the path.

room.name: a collapsed air-raid shelter.
room.description: Concrete steps lead down into darkness, but rubble blocks the way. A faded sign reads 'SHELTER'. Perhaps with tools or persistence, it could be cleared one day.

room.name: Victory Square.
room.description: Once known as Trafalgar Square, this place is now a monument to Party control. Nelson's Column is gone, replaced by a towering statue of Big Brother, his eyes watching every movement. The facades of buildings are cracked and peeling, plastered with propaganda posters. A few Proles mill about, under the gaze of ever-present telescreens.

room.name: Holborn Crossing.
room.description: The wide junction of cracked streets, once a hub of London. Rubble and posters choke the corners, and telescreens bark orders from the lampposts.

room.name: Piccadilly Circus.
room.description: Once a bustling landmark, the square now lies hollow under the Party's gaze. The statue of Eros is gone, replaced by a plinth topped with gallows and noose. Public executions are frequent. Telescreens look out in all directions from below the gallows. Storefronts are stripped bare except for official Party shops: Victory Gin, Victory Shoes, Victory Coffee. Propaganda posters peel from the walls, their slogans half-lost to the rain. A few Inner Party members pass briskly, ignoring the ruin.

room.name: Kingsway, near Aldwych.
room.description: The street slopes gently down, lined with grand but ruined facades. Most windows are broken, and the few intact doors are painted with Party symbols. Eastward, the road curves toward Aldwych.

room.name: what used to be Aldwych by the Strand.
room.description: Once a fashionable crescent, now a desolate square of cracked pavement and guards patrolling around the Ministry. The Ministry of Truth stands before you a huge, gleaming white pyramidal edifice.

room.name: Gray's Inn Road.
room.description: A long, straight road heading northeast. Bombed-out terraces stand beside Party offices with loudspeakers nailed above their doors. Few linger here - the Ministry’s shadow looms ahead.

room.name: Pentonville.
room.description: The streets grow darker, narrower, more oppressive. Concrete walls cut across where old roads once ran. Ahead, barbed wire and watchtowers guard a massive block of stone - the Ministry of Love.

room.name: Whitehall.
room.description: Once the administrative heart of government, Whitehall now lies beneath the shadow of the Ministries. The tradition stone columns rise on either side, their facades scarred by decades of "restoration work." Party slogans "AUSTERITY IS LIBERTY", "WAR IS PEACE" - are plastered over boarded windows. A slow procession of blue-overalled clerks shuffles between buildings, heads bowed, eyes averted from the omnipresent telescreens mounted on lamp posts. Occasionally, a figure in black overalls glides past - an Inner Party official - and conversation dies until they've gone. Somewhere far above, a loudspeaker crackles, broadcasting a speech about increased production quotas in the Munitions Department.

room.name: Miilbank.
room.description: The wide avenue curves south beside the river, its once-grand embassies converted into Party bureaus. The air smells faintly of oil and disinfectant. Armoured trucks idle along the kerb, their engines rumbling softly while uniformed officers inspect passers-by for papers. Beyond the checkpoints, the skyline is a tangle of cranes and half-finished Ministry extensions. A poster on a brick wall peels in the damp wind - the face of Big Brother, his eyes following you wherever you move.

room.name: Vauxhall Bridge.
room.description: The river glints darkly beneath the bridge, carrying debris and the faint smell of chemicals downstream. Patrol boats drift between the piers, their searchlights sweeping lazily across the water. Beyond the eastern bank, you can just make out the shell of a disused theatre - its roof sagging, its once-bright façade blackened with soot. Somewhere nearby, a burst of static from a telescreen fades into silence. The air feels colder here, thinner, as though the city itself is holding its breath. Armoured trucks idle along the kerb, their engines rumbling softly while uniformed officers inspect passers-by for papers. Beyond the checkpoints, the skyline is a tangle of cranes and half-finished Ministry extensions. A poster on a brick wall peels in the damp wind - the face of Big Brother, his eyes following you wherever you move.
